channel.title: Saurabh Jain
channel.description: Security engineer writing about cloud security, bug bounties, AWS, and the grey areas no one puts on a slide deck.

item.title: Unauthenticated Access to AWS via Cognito Identity Pool ID
item.description: How an exposed Cognito Identity Pool ID can hand attackers temporary AWS credentials — no login required.

item.title: The Day Google Stopped Helping Me at Work
item.description: When you get promoted, you stop fixing bugs and start choosing battles. No StackOverflow for that.

item.title: 20+ Vulnerabilities in a Static Website
item.description: No database, no backend, no APIs — still 20+ findings. Here's the bug bounty cheat sheet for static sites.

item.title: Built a Python Tool to Automate Email Security Audits
item.description: Automating SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and MTA-STS checks with a simple Python script.

item.title: Security Fundamentals for Beginners
item.description: Nine foundational security principles that matter more than any tool or framework.

item.title: Recon to Responsible Disclosure
item.description: From rejected duplicates and out-of-scope reports to a methodical recon process that actually works.

item.title: Prompting Cheat Sheet for Security Engineers
item.description: Practical prompting templates for security engineers who want AI to actually be useful.

item.title: How to Be a Good Prompter
item.description: Unlocking clarity, creativity, and better outputs from AI through one simple rule: the more you tell, the better you get.

item.title: The Last Time You'll Ever Use a Password
item.description: Passkeys are here. Here's what they are, how they work, and why passwords are finally dying.

item.title: Cracked AWS Cloud Practitioner in 2 Weeks
item.description: How I passed AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner (CLF-C02) in two weeks with prior hands-on experience and a focused study plan.

item.title: Security Engineer Interview Questions Part-6
item.description: Part 6 of the series covering security engineer interview questions, including AWS IMDSv2 security enhancements and why CORS alone cannot prevent CSRF attacks.

item.title: OhSINT TryHackMe Walkthrough
item.description: A walkthrough of the OhSINT room on TryHackMe, demonstrating how to extract EXIF metadata from an image file and use OSINT techniques to answer challenge questions.

item.title: Security Engineer Interview Questions Part-5
item.description: Part 5 of the series presents a vulnerable Java code snippet and walks through identifying multiple security flaws including SQL injection, hardcoded credentials, and missing input validation.

item.title: Security Engineer Interview Questions Part-3
item.description: Part 3 of the series covers AWS network architecture, specifically where to place a Load Balancer and S3 Bucket within public and private subnets for optimal security.

item.title: Security Engineer Interview Questions Part-2
item.description: Part 2 of the series explains the differences between monolithic and microservice architectures from a security and scalability perspective.

item.title: Security Engineer Interview Questions Part-1
item.description: Part 1 of a multi-part series sharing real security engineer interview questions from major tech companies, covering the correct sequencing of WAF, IPS, and IDS in a network architecture.

item.title: Resolving Proxy Issue in Genymotion
item.description: A quick guide to working around the broken Advanced Options WiFi proxy configuration in Genymotion 3.7.1 by using ADB commands instead.

item.title: Integrating Shodan and Censys API Keys into Subfinder
item.description: A step-by-step guide to enhancing Subfinder with Shodan, Censys, and SecurityTrails API keys to dramatically increase subdomain discovery coverage during recon.

item.title: Security Beyond Browsers: Why CORS Doesn't Apply to Mobile Applications
item.description: CORS is a browser-enforced security mechanism — not a server or OS feature. This article explains why native mobile apps are immune to CORS restrictions and what that means for security testing.

item.title: AgentSudo Write-Up: TryHackMe Machine Walk-through
item.description: A walkthrough of the AgentSudo machine on TryHackMe, a Linux box requiring exploitation of information disclosure vulnerabilities and a sudo authentication bypass (CVE-2019-14287) for privilege escalation.

item.title: BountyHunter Walkthrough: HackTheBox Writeup
item.description: BountyHunter is a beginner Linux machine on HackTheBox. This walkthrough covers nmap recon, exploiting an XXE vulnerability in a bug report form to read local files, and privilege escalation via a Python sudo script.

item.title: TomGhost Write-Up: TryHackMe Walk-through
item.description: A walkthrough of the TomGhost easy Linux machine on TryHackMe, exploiting Apache Tomcat via the Apache JServ Protocol (AJP) Ghostcat vulnerability (CVE-2020-1938).

item.title: Cap Walk-through: HackTheBox Machine Write-up
item.description: Cap is a beginner Linux machine on HackTheBox. This walkthrough covers nmap recon, discovering a PCAP download vulnerability exposing FTP credentials, and privilege escalation via Linux capabilities.

item.title: BackDoor 1: NET-SQUARE Hacking Warm-Up Mobile Application Challenge
item.description: Walkthrough of the BackDoor 1 mobile CTF challenge from NET-SQUARE — extracting hidden credentials from an Android APK using static reverse engineering tools.

item.title: BackDoor 2: NET-SQUARE Hacking Warm-Up Mobile Application Challenge
item.description: Walkthrough of BackDoor 2 from NET-SQUARE's CTF series — credentials are encoded rather than plaintext, requiring a deeper static analysis to locate and decode them.

item.title: BackDoor 3: NET-SQUARE Hacking Warm-Up Mobile Application Challenge
item.description: Walkthrough of BackDoor 3 — the most complex challenge in NET-SQUARE's series, requiring multi-step extraction combining static analysis, runtime instrumentation, and potentially native library inspection.

item.title: Morty Sherlocked: Android Application CTF Challenge Walkthrough
item.description: A beginner-level Android CTF challenge walkthrough covering APK analysis, reverse engineering, and common mobile security vulnerabilities.

item.title: Morty's New Tool: Android Application CTF Challenge Walkthrough
item.description: An intermediate Android CTF challenge involving dynamic code transformation at runtime, native library reverse engineering, and Frida instrumentation to intercept a hidden flag.

item.title: Planet X: Android Application CTF Challenge Walkthrough
item.description: Walkthrough of the Planet X Android CTF challenge — a beginner-friendly challenge teaching foundational mobile application security assessment techniques.

item.title: Universe Weird C132: Android Application CTF Challenge Walkthrough
item.description: Walkthrough of the Universe Weird C132 Android CTF challenge, covering reverse engineering techniques and common mobile application security flaws.
